text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY
OFFICE VISIT NOTE — FOLLOW-UP

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: GU Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old Polish-American male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) harboring germline and somatic BRCA2 biallelic loss, presenting for follow-up after a recent hospitalization-equivalent encounter in the infusion center for Grade 3 anemia requiring packed red blood cell transfusion and a dose hold of olaparib. He is here to reassess blood counts, review PSA, and determine readiness to resume olaparib at a further reduced dose.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski was initially diagnosed with Stage IVB prostatic adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5, T3bN1M1b) approximately 3 years ago at age 68. His disease was characterized by high-volume bone metastases (L3, L4, right iliac crest, right sacral ala), pelvic lymph node involvement, and a presenting PSA of 187.4 ng/mL. He was started on ADT with leuprolide acetate plus abiraterone/prednisone for metastatic hormone-sensitive disease and had an excellent initial response — PSA nadir of 0.8 ng/mL — before developing castration-resistant disease at approximately 24 months, with a PSA doubling time of 3.5 months at that point.

Germline testing performed during the hormone-sensitive phase identified a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift variant (c.5946delT), and somatic NGS from a CT-guided biopsy of the L3 vertebral metastasis at the time of CRPC confirmed biallelic BRCA2 loss through LOH of the germline allele plus somatic deletion of the second allele, along with AR amplification (~8x copy number gain), TP53 p.R175H, and RB1 homozygous deletion — a constellation suggestive of highly aggressive disease biology.

He was treated with enzalutamide 160 mg daily as second-line AR-pathway therapy, achieving an initial PSA decline to 1.9 ng/mL, but progressed rapidly at approximately 7 months with new bone lesions at the right femoral neck/intertrochanteric region, T11, and left acetabulum, plus two new small PSMA-avid right pelvic lymph nodes on Ga-68 PSMA PET/CT. PSA at progression was in the range of 18.2 ng/mL.

Given his BRCA2 biallelic loss and FDA-approved indication (PROfound trial), he was transitioned to olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg orally twice daily approximately 8 months ago, with concurrent initiation of denosumab 120 mg SQ every 4 weeks for skeletal event prevention, and continuation of leuprolide for testosterone suppression.

His initial olaparib response was favorable: PSA declined from 18.2 to 5.7 ng/mL at 3 months (a 69% reduction), with significant improvement in bone pain (reported as 1-2/10 from a prior peak of 4-5/10 on enzalutamide), and partial radiographic response on 6-month restaging scans (reduced tracer uptake at right femoral neck, T11, and left acetabulum; right pelvic lymph nodes decreased to 0.4 cm).

However, his course has been complicated by progressive olaparib-associated myelosuppression. His hemoglobin declined from a baseline of 12.4 g/dL to 10.8 g/dL by month 3, prompting a dose reduction to 200 mg BID at month 4 for Grade 2 anemia. Despite this, he developed Grade 3 anemia (Hgb 7.4 g/dL) at approximately 7 months, accompanied by Grade 1 thrombocytopenia (platelets 128 K/uL) and symptomatic fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, and lightheadedness. He received 2 units of packed red blood cells in the infusion center, with a post-transfusion hemoglobin of 9.8 g/dL, and olaparib was held at that time. He is now presenting approximately 3-4 weeks following that transfusion event for reassessment.

Since the dose hold, Mr. Kowalski reports his energy level has partially recovered. He notes he is less short of breath with moderate exertion (no longer dyspneic walking from parking to clinic). He continues to have mild fatigue (his words: "I'm not 100%, but I'm doing okay — better than last month"). No active bone pain at rest; mild aching in the lower back and right hip with extended walking, which he rates 1-2/10. He denies any new neurological symptoms, chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or fall events. He reports adequate oral intake and no nausea at present. No fever, chills, or signs of infection. He has been taking folic acid supplementation as recommended. His wife accompanies him today.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Mild fatigue (Grade 1, improved from Grade 2-3 prior to transfusion). No fever, chills, night sweats. Mild unintentional weight loss over the past several months, approximately 5-6 lbs total; weight appears to have stabilized recently per patient report.
- HEENT: No oral mucositis, dysphagia, or visual changes.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or edema. No prior thromboembolic events.
- Pulmonary: Mild exertional dyspnea, improved since transfusion (Grade 1). No cough, hemoptysis.
- GI: Appetite reduced but functional. No nausea/vomiting currently. No melena, hematochezia, or change in bowel habits.
- GU: No gross hematuria. No urinary obstruction symptoms (on chronic ADT; voiding without significant difficulty).
- Musculoskeletal: Mild low back and right hip aching 1-2/10 with activity.
- Neurological: No dizziness at rest; had lightheadedness prior to transfusion, resolved. No seizures. No paresthesias beyond mild pre-existing from prior docetaxel (fingers, feet; Grade 1).
- Hematologic: History of transfusion-dependent anemia as above. No spontaneous bleeding.
- Endocrine/Metabolic: Chronic hot flashes from ADT (Grade 1-2, pre-existing). No hyperhidrosis beyond usual.
- Skin: No alopecia currently (on PARP inhibitor, not taxane).
- Psychiatric: Patient reports some anxiety about disease course and treatment options; manageable; engaging with palliative care team.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate adenocarcinoma (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, BRCA2 biallelic loss — as detailed above
2. Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22) — identified during hormone-sensitive phase; family counseling completed
3. Androgen deprivation therapy-related sequelae: osteoporosis risk (on denosumab), hot flashes, fatigue, reduced libido
4. Hypertension — well controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily (was monitored on abiraterone given mineralocorticoid excess risk; currently not on abiraterone)
5. Chronic kidney disease stage 3a (GFR 52 mL/min at last check) — influenced bone agent selection (denosumab over zoledronic acid)
6. Mild peripheral neuropathy — Grade 1, attributed to prior docetaxel exposure during the hormone-sensitive phase (though he did not receive docetaxel in the hormone-sensitive setting — this reflects prior history of taxane exposure at CRPC diagnosis workup notes; clinical neuropathy is Grade 1 at baseline pre-olaparib)

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
- No prior prostate surgery (patient was treated with medical management only; no TURP, no prostatectomy)
- No other major surgical procedures reported

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Father: Prostate cancer, died age 78
- Paternal aunt: Ovarian cancer
- BRCA2 germline pathogenic variant identified in patient; first-degree relatives (1 adult son, 1 adult daughter) have been counseled and offered cascade testing

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Retired ironworker; worked in construction and steel fabrication for ~35 years
- Non-smoker (never)
- Occasional alcohol use in the past; none currently
- Married, lives with wife; she is his primary caregiver
- Lives in a single-level home; managed independently with assistance from wife
- ECOG PS: 1 (baseline); currently transitioning between 1 and 1+ depending on fatigue burden and anemia
- No relevant toxic exposures beyond occupational (asbestos exposure uncertain — worked with structural steel; no prior asbestos-related pulmonary disease)

ALLERGIES:
- No known drug allergies
- No known food allergies

MEDICATIONS (current):
1. Olaparib (Lynparza) — currently HELD (last dose approximately 3-4 weeks ago at 200 mg BID; will reassess today for resumption at 150 mg BID)
2. Leuprolide acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM every 3 months — for testosterone suppression; last injection approximately 6 weeks ago; next due in ~6 weeks
3. Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg SQ every 4 weeks — for bone metastasis skeletal event prevention; last given approximately 3 weeks ago
4. Calcium carbonate 500 mg orally daily — with denosumab
5. Vitamin D3 400 IU orally daily — with denosumab
6. Folic acid 1 mg orally daily — initiated for olaparib-related hematologic support
7. Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily — for hypertension
8. Ondansetron 4 mg orally PRN — for nausea (using infrequently currently)
9. Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg orally PRN — for bone pain (using occasionally)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 88 bpm (improved from 104 at last visit), RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air, Weight 176 lbs (stable from last visit, down ~6 lbs from pre-olaparib baseline of 182 lbs), Temperature 98.2°F.

General: Alert and oriented x3. Appears comfortable at rest. Mildly fatigued-appearing but in no acute distress. Better color than reported at last acute encounter; conjunctival pallor now mild rather than pronounced.

HEENT: Anicteric sclerae. Moist mucous membranes. Oropharynx clear. No oral ulcers.

Neck: Supple. No lymphadenopathy appreciated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No S3/S4. No JVD. Trace lower extremity edema, bilateral, pitting, symmetric (Grade 1; noted to be chronic, possibly ADT-related). Distal pulses intact.

Pulmonary: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing or crackles.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Normal bowel sounds. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated. No RUQ tenderness.

Musculoskeletal: Mild tenderness to palpation over the lumbar spine (L3-L4 region), consistent with known metastatic disease at those levels; no focal neurologic deficits. No point tenderness over right femoral neck.

Neurological: Cranial nerves grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in upper extremities, 4+/5 in lower extremities (baseline, secondary to fatigue and deconditioning). Gait steady but mildly cautious. Deep tendon reflexes 1+ at patella bilaterally. Mild decreased sensation to light touch bilateral feet (Grade 1, consistent with prior history).

Skin: No rash, petechiae, ecchymoses, or purpura. No alopecia.

Rectal/Prostate: Deferred (not clinically indicated at this visit; patient managed with medical therapy).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today, pre-visit draw):

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 4.6 K/uL (normal; no neutropenia)
- Hemoglobin: 9.6 g/dL (Grade 1-2 anemia; improved from nadir of 7.4 g/dL pre-transfusion; post-transfusion peak was 9.8 g/dL — largely maintained)
- Hematocrit: 29.1%
- MCV: 95 fL (mild macrocytosis, improved from 98 fL at time of acute anemia; resolving megaloblastic pattern)
- Platelets: 118 K/uL (borderline low-normal; previously 128 K/uL at Grade 3 anemia event; stable)
- Reticulocyte count: 0.9% (improved from 0.3% at nadir; recovering erythropoiesis)
- Absolute reticulocyte count: ~45 K/uL (still suboptimal but improving)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL (estimated GFR ~52 mL/min — stable), BUN 22 mg/dL
- ALT 32 U/L (normal), AST 38 U/L (borderline), Alkaline phosphatase 148 U/L (elevated, consistent with bone metastases), Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL
- Albumin 3.6 g/dL (mildly low)
- Calcium 9.1 mg/dL (normal; on denosumab)

PSA: 4.4 ng/mL
- For context: PSA was 18.2 ng/mL at olaparib initiation, declined to nadir of approximately 3.5-3.9 ng/mL at the 6-month scan, and has risen slightly to 4.4 ng/mL during the recent dose hold and anemia management period.

Testosterone: <10 ng/dL (confirmed castrate)

Iron Studies: Serum iron 72 mcg/dL, Ferritin 295 ng/mL, TIBC normal — no iron deficiency; pattern consistent with anemia of chronic disease / PARP inhibitor-associated marrow suppression, not iron deficiency anemia.

Vitamin B12: 412 pg/mL (normal). Folate: 8.2 ng/mL (normal on supplementation).

---

IMAGING:
Most recent imaging: Tc-99m bone scintigraphy and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 2 months ago (at the 6-month olaparib mark) showed partial radiographic response — reduced tracer uptake at right femoral neck, T11, and left acetabulum; sclerotic/stable lesions at L3, L4, right iliac crest; right pelvic lymph nodes decreased from 0.8 cm to 0.4 cm; no visceral metastases. PSA at that scan was 3.9 ng/mL. No new imaging obtained today; restaging deferred pending clinical stabilization and dose re-establishment.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with heavily pretreated mCRPC — BRCA2 biallelic loss, AR amplified — now approximately 8 months into olaparib therapy, with an excellent PSA and imaging response that has been complicated by progressive and ultimately Grade 3 olaparib-related anemia requiring two dose reductions and a dose hold, as well as packed red blood cell transfusion. Today's visit is focused on:

1. OLAPARIB-RELATED ANEMIA / MYELOSUPPRESSION:
Hemoglobin today is 9.6 g/dL, which represents stabilization following the transfusion approximately 3-4 weeks ago (post-transfusion Hgb was 9.8 g/dL; largely maintained, with minimal expected cell senescence accounting for the marginal difference). Reticulocyte count at 0.9% is an encouraging trend from the nadir of 0.3%, suggesting early erythropoietic recovery with the dose hold. Platelets at 118 K/uL are borderline but stable, not requiring intervention.

Given that the Hgb is now 9.6 g/dL and stable, meets our threshold of >9 g/dL for resumption as previously discussed, the plan is to restart olaparib at 150 mg orally twice daily — a further reduction from the 200 mg BID at which he experienced Grade 3 anemia. This is consistent with the olaparib prescribing information dose modification guidelines for hematologic toxicity.

We will additionally initiate darbepoetin alfa (Aranesp) 500 mcg subcutaneously every 3 weeks to support erythropoiesis in the context of ongoing PARP inhibitor-associated myelosuppression. The rationale for ESA use here is the transfusion-dependent anemia in a non-curative setting where the goal is to maintain meaningful hemoglobin levels to support quality of life and allow continued treatment. The Hgb target will be 10-12 g/dL; we will not push above 12 g/dL given thromboembolic risk, particularly given his immobility. Iron stores are adequate (ferritin 295, TIBC normal), so IV iron supplementation is not indicated at this time.

CBC will be rechecked in 2 weeks and then every 3 weeks thereafter aligned with darbepoetin dosing schedule. Patient and wife counseled on signs of severe anemia requiring urgent evaluation (syncope, severe dyspnea, chest pain).

2. PSA / DISEASE STATUS:
PSA today is 4.4 ng/mL. This is a slight rise from the 6-month post-olaparib scan value of 3.9 ng/mL, which I attribute primarily to the dose hold period over the last 3-4 weeks rather than clear disease progression. The overall trajectory from baseline PSA of 18.2 ng/mL at olaparib initiation to current 4.4 ng/mL represents a durable and meaningful PSA response (~76% decline from baseline) in the context of BRCA2-driven PARP inhibitor sensitivity. I do not believe the 4.4 ng/mL value, in isolation, represents biochemical progression at this time — we will monitor closely once olaparib is re-established.

No restaging imaging today. Next imaging planned in approximately 6-8 weeks from olaparib resumption, or sooner if PSA rises substantially or new symptoms develop.

3. DENOSUMAB / BONE HEALTH:
Continue Xgeva 120 mg SQ every 4 weeks. Calcium 9.1 mg/dL — normal on current supplementation. No jaw pain reported; last dental clearance approximately 14 months ago — would recommend dental check within the next 2-3 months given ongoing denosumab therapy.

4. LEUPROLIDE / TESTOSTERONE SUPPRESSION:
Testosterone confirmed castrate at <10 ng/dL. Next leuprolide depot injection due in approximately 6 weeks — flagged for nursing staff to schedule.

5. PERFORMANCE STATUS AND SUPPORTIVE CARE:
ECOG PS 1 today (improved from PS 1-2 at the acute anemia event). Fatigue is Grade 1, manageable. Palliative care team is engaged and actively following. Physical therapy referral discussed for deconditioning and lower extremity strength given prior immobility from anemia and bone pain — patient agreeable. Nutritional counseling referral placed given mild hypoalbuminemia (3.6 g/dL) and 5-6 lb weight loss.

6. GOALS OF CARE:
Ongoing conversation. Patient and wife understand the disease trajectory. Patient has expressed a clear desire to continue treatment and has maintained good insight into his disease. We have briefly discussed that if olaparib becomes intolerable or if disease progresses, further options exist (including platinum-based chemotherapy, other clinical trials), though we did not go into detail today as the focus was on re-establishing current therapy. Advance directive in place per chart.

RETURN TO CLINIC:
- In 2 weeks for repeat CBC (hemoglobin and platelet monitoring after olaparib re-initiation and first darbepoetin alfa injection today)
- Full follow-up visit in 4 weeks with PSA, CMP, CBC, and clinical assessment
- Imaging restaging approximately 6-8 weeks after olaparib resumption

Time spent: approximately 50 minutes including counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed by:
Attending, Genitourinary Medical Oncology
